coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of a ternary BiOCl/CQDs/rGO photocatalyst: The roles of CQDs and rGO in adsorption-photocatalytic removal of ciprofloxacin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Jiehong
Chen, Weizhen
Yu, Xiang
Fu, Xionghui
Zhu, Yi
Zhang, Yuanming
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a ternary BiOCl/carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/reduced graphene oxide (rGO) composite photocatalyst with high removal efficiency of ciprofloxacin was fabricated. For BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10, rGO greatly enhanced the adsorption capacity, while rGO and CQDs significantly improved the photocatalytic performance by enhancing the charge separation and charge injection, which were 3.8 and 8.4 times higher than those of BiOCl, respectively. Interestingly, the photon absorption rate of BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 was only 1.3 times higher than that of BiOCl, suggesting that CQDs and rGO could not improve the effective light absorption although they could enhance the light harvesting ability and demonstrating the enhancement in light harvesting ability was not an essential factor for improving photocatalytic activity. In addition, BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 displayed high stability and the photocatalytic mechanism of ciprofloxacin was also proposed. This work not only exhibited an excellent reference with outstanding adsorption-photocatalytic performance in application of water remediation, but also provided a new insight into the roles of CQDs and rGO in the adsorption and photocatalysis for CQDs or rGO based composite photocatalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: BiOCl/CQDs/rGO
Adsorption
Photocatalysis
Ciprofloxacin
Visible light

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of BiOCl, BiOCl/CQDs and BiOCl/CQDs/rGO.
FT-IR spectra of BiOCl, BiOCl/CQDs, BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 and GO.
SEM images of (a) BiOCl and (b) BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10. (c, d) TEM images and (e–h) EDS images of BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10.
(a) XPS survey spectra, (b) Bi 4f, (c) Cl 2p, (d) C 1s and (e) O 1s of the obtained samples and GO.
Raman spectra of GO, BiOCl/rGO-10 and BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10.
UV–vis DRS of BiOCl, BiOCl/CQDs and BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10.
Time-dependent adsorption of CIP by BiOCl, BiOCl/CQDs and BiOCl/CQDs/rGO.
(a) Adsorption-photocatalytic removal of CIP and (b) first-order rate constants of CIP degradation by BiOCl, BiOCl/CQDs and BiOCl/CQDs/rGO.
(a) Recycling tests of CIP removal by BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 and (b) XRD patterns of the fresh and reused BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10.
Photocurrent responses in the aqueous solution containing (a) only Na2SO4 and (b) Na2SO4 and K2S2O8 of BiOCl, BiOCl/CQDs and BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10.
(a) PL spectra and (b) Nyquist plots of EIS of BiOCl, BiOCl/CQDs and BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10.
Effect of reactive species on photocatalytic degradation of CIP by BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10.
Schematic illustration of the photocatalytic mechanism of CIP by BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10.
Proposed photocatalytic degradation pathway of CIP.
Comparison of the removal efficiencies of CIP with reported photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of a ternary BiOCl/CQDs/rGO photocatalyst: The roles of CQDs and rGO in adsorption-photocatalytic removal of ciprofloxacin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Jinan University, Guangzhou 510632, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Jinan University, Guangzhou 510632, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical & Testing Center, Jinan University, Guangzhou 510632, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Analytical & Testing Center, Jinan University, Guangzhou 510632, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 exhibited excellent adsorption-photocatalytic removal of CIP due to the introduction of CQDs and rGO.

In this work, a ternary BiOCl/carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/reduced graphene oxide (rGO) composite photocatalyst with high removal efficiency of ciprofloxacin was fabricated. For BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10, rGO greatly enhanced the adsorption capacity, while rGO and CQDs significantly improved the photocatalytic performance by enhancing the charge separation and charge injection, which were 3.8 and 8.4 times higher than those of BiOCl, respectively. Interestingly, the photon absorption rate of BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 was only 1.3 times higher than that of BiOCl, suggesting that CQDs and rGO could not improve the effective light absorption although they could enhance the light harvesting ability and demonstrating the enhancement in light harvesting ability was not an essential factor for improving photocatalytic activity. In addition, BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 displayed high stability and the photocatalytic mechanism of ciprofloxacin was also proposed. This work not only exhibited an excellent reference with outstanding adsorption-photocatalytic performance in application of water remediation, but also provided a new insight into the roles of CQDs and rGO in the adsorption and photocatalysis for CQDs or rGO based composite photocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, a variety of antibiotics have been widely applied in the treatment of bacterial infections, the extensive use of which can induce toxicological effects on aquatic organisms [1–3]. Thus, various technologies have been developed to eliminate antibiotics [4–6]. Adsorption is a conventional method for its easy operation, but it only achieves the phase transfer of antibiotics and produces new solid residue [7,8]. Electrolysis is effective to remove pollutant, yet the stability is poor [9]. Advanced oxidation processes based on the sulfate radical have the advantage of high oxidizing ability [10]. However, sulfate leaving in these processes is harmful to the environment [11]. Nowadays, photocatalysis is considered as a promising way for the treatment of antibiotics owing to its low cost, environmental friendliness, strong oxidation ability and photostability [12–14]. Among the numerous photocatalysts, BiOCl has gained great interest for its layered structure consisting of [Bi2O2] slabs interleaved with two slabs of halogen atoms, which is able to form inherent static electric fields by the difference in electronegativities [15]. Such structure of BiOCl can efficiently suppress the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers, and thus improve photocatalytic activity [16–18]. However, the photocatalytic efficiency is still too low and the band gap is too wide, which limit the practical application on a large scale [19,20]. A variety of strategies have been employed to solve the problems, including elemental doping, defect introduction, semiconductor heterojunction and interface engineering with noble metals or carbon materials [21,22]. As the promising photocatalysts, BiOCl/carbon-based material photocatalysts have gained enormous interest for their enhanced photocatalytic activities by strong adsorption capacities, excellent light absorption and rapid transfer of photogenerated electrons.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been explored in modification of photocatalysts for their efficient optical absorption, unique photoluminescence and rapid photoinduced electron transfer [23]. For the composite photocatalysts such as TiO2/CQDs [24], g-C3N4/CQDs [25,26], ZnO/CQDs [27] and BiOCl/CQDs [28], CQDs play crucial roles in improvement of light absorption and electron transfer, thus enhancing the photocatalytic activities of the photocatalysts.

It is widely known that enrichment of organic pollutants on photocatalysts by adsorption is very important because it’s the first step in the photocatalytic degradation process. Nevertheless, the adsorption capacities of semiconductor/CQDs photocatalysts are unsatisfactory. Modifying photocatalysts by reduced graphene oxide (rGO) is an effective way to improve the adsorption property owing to its high surface area [29]. What’s more, rGO exhibits high mobility of photogenerated electrons from the semiconductor, which reduces the recombination of charge carriers and ultimately enhances the photocatalytic activity [30]. Various semiconductor/rGO photocatalysts with high adsorption capacities and excellent photocatalytic performances have been reported such as TiO2/rGO [31,32], Ag3PO4/rGO [33] and Bi2Fe4O9/rGO [34].

It is reasonable to modify photocatalysts with CQDs and rGO to achieve extraordinary photocatalytic activity by combining the advantages of rGO and CQDs. For example, He et al. reported a ternary system g-C3N4/CQDs/rGO synthesized via a two-step hydrothermal method, which showed superior removal efficiency of methyl orange [35]. Huo and his coworkers fabricated visible-light-driven CdSe/CQDs/rGO to achieve the excellent photodegradation performance towards tetracycline hydrochloride [36].

However, the specific contributions of CQDs and rGO to the enhancement of photocatalytic activity have never been quantified. Moreover, photocatalytic performance depends on the effective light absorption, charge separation and charge injection, whereas the roles of CQDs and rGO have never been quantified in terms of these factors.

In this work, we incorporated CQDs and rGO with BiOCl to fabricate a ternary visible-light-driven BiOCl/CQDs/rGO photocatalyst. Under visible-light illumination, the prepared BiOCl/CQDs/rGO showed significantly enhanced removal efficiency of ciprofloxacin (CIP) compared with pristine BiOCl, which was attributed to the excellent adsorption-photocatalytic activity. The adsorption experiments of CIP were carried out to confirm the beneficial influence of rGO on the adsorption ability. Additionally, the roles of CQDs and rGO in photocatalysis were further evaluated in terms of the effective light absorption, charge separation and charge injection. A reasonable photocatalytic mechanism of CIP was also proposed on the basis of band structures, free radicals trapping experiments and photoelectrochemical properties.

Sucrose (AR), Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (AR, 99.0 %), mannitol (AR, 98.0 %) and triethanolamine (AR, 98 %) were purchased from Aladdin. NaCl (GR, 99.8 %), K2S2O8 (AR, 99.5 %) and benzoquinone (97 %) were purchased from Macklin. Graphene oxide (99.5 %) was purchased from Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Na2SO4 (AR, ≥99 %) and ethanol (AR) were purchased from Meilunbio and Tianjin Damao Reagent, respectively. Ciprofloxacin (88.5 %) and isopropanol (≥99.7 %) were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd and Guangzhou Chemical Reagent, respectively. Deionized water was used for all experiments.
CQDs were fabricated according to a facile pyrolysis method reported before [37] and then dissolved into ethanol (30 mL) for further use.

BiOCl/CQDs was prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method [38]. Typically, 2 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was added into 55 mL mannitol solution (0.1 M) to obtain a transparent solution, followed by adding 10 mL saturated NaCl solution dropwise. After stirring for 30 min, 2 mL CQDs solution was added into the uniform white suspension, which was then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave, heated to 150 °C for 3 h and cooled to room temperature. Subsequently, the resultant precipitate was collected, washed with deionized water and ethanol, and then dried at 60 °C. Pure BiOCl was prepared by following the same procedure without adding the CQDs solution.

The preparation of BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-5 (the mass ratio of rGO:BiOCl/CQDs was 5:100) was as followed. 17 mL ethanol was added into 17 mL graphene oxide (GO) aqueous solution and ultrasonically treated for 1 h. Then 0.2 g BiOCl/CQDs was added into the obtained GO solution, sonicated for 30 min and stirred for 2 h. The suspension was placed into a sealed autoclave and kept at 120 °C for 3 h. After cooling down naturally, the collected products were thoroughly washed with distilled water and dried at 60 °C. Other samples BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-x (x = 10, 15) were prepared by the same steps just with addition of different weights of GO. The synthesis procedure is shown in Fig. S1. BiOCl/rGO-10 was also synthesized by the same method with BiOCl instead of BiOCl/CQDs being added.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was detected using Rigaku MiniFlex 600 analyzer. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Zeiss ULTRA 55) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEOL JEM 2100 F) were used to characterize the morphologies of samples. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was investigated on an ESCALab-250 spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra and Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were obtained using a SHIMADZU IR Prestige21 FTIR spectrometer and a SHIMADZU RF-5301 PC spectrometer with excitation wavelength at 350 nm, respectively. The UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra (DRS) and Raman spectra were determined by a SHIMADZU UV-2600 spectrophotometer and LabRAM HR Evolution with a 633 nm excitation, respectively. The electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) measurement was conducted using a Bruker A300 spectrometer. Liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC–MS, Waters SQD2) and thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, Mettler TGA/DSC3+) were also performed.

An electrochemical workstation (CHI 760D) was used to obtain the electrochemical experimental results on a three-electrode cell, which was composed of the sample (working electrode), Pt wire (counter electrode) and Ag/AgCl (reference electrode). The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was collected in 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution. The photocurrent measurements were performed in 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution with or without 0.1 M K2S2O8 under visible light (a 300 W Xe arc lamp with 420 nm cutoff filter).

Typically, 25 mg samples were added into 20 mg/L CIP solution (50 mL) with continuously stirring in dark and 3 mL aliquots were sampled at certain time intervals. The concentrations of CIP were detected by the UV–vis spectrophotometer (TU-1901).

The photocatalytic performance was investigated by photodegradation of CIP. In every experiment, 25 mg materials were added to 20 mg/L CIP solution (50 mL) with continuously stirring. The mixture was kept stirring for 1 h in dark to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium, and then a 300 W Xe arc lamp with 420 nm cutoff filter was used as the visible light source. 3 mL aliquots were collected every 20 min and the concentrations of CIP were analyzed by the UV–vis spectrophotometer.

The pseudo-first order model was applied to investigate the reaction kinetics:
-
ln
(
C t
/
C 0
)
= k t
where k was the kinetics constant, and C0 and Ct were the concentrations of CIP at times 0 and t.
To investigate the main active species in photocatalysis, photodegradation of CIP with different scavengers including isopropanol (IPA), triethanolamine (TEOA) and benzoquinone (BQ) were carried out.

The crystal phases of all samples were characterized by XRD (Fig. 1 ). The characteristic peaks of all samples well corresponded to the BiOCl with tetragonal phase (JCPDS No. 06-0249) [38], indicating the good crystallinity and the high purity of the products. However, for BiOCl/CQDs and BiOCl/CQDs/rGO, the peaks of CQDs and rGO could not be observed, which was due to their low contents. Moreover, no differences on the relative diffraction intensity and no peaks shift were founded, suggesting that CQDs and rGO would not affect the crystal phases of BiOCl.

The FT-IR spectra were analyzed to confirm the presence of CQDs and rGO (Fig. 2 ). Compared with BiOCl, BiOCl/CQDs displayed a new band at 1380 cm−1,which might be caused by CH bending vibration of introduced CQDs [39]. In BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10, two new bands could be also observed at 1239 cm−1 for COC stretching and at 1721 cm−1 for CO stretching vibration [40], which were consistent with the characteristic bands of GO, demonstrating both CQDs and rGO existed in BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10.

The morphologies of BiOCl and BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 were investigated by SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 3 a, pure BiOCl was mainly composed of uniform nanosheets with diameters about 50–200 nm. For BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10, SEM images (Fig. 3b) clearly indicated the BiOCl/CQDs nanosheets were wrapped by thin layers of rGO. While the quantum-sized of CQDs couldn’t be distinguished from the images because of the resolution limit of the instrument. The corresponding TEM images (Fig. 3c, d) presented that BiOCl/CQDs nanosheets were uniformly distributed on the rGO layers. The lattice spacing of 0.276 nm and 0.324 nm corresponded with (110) planes of BiOCl [38] and (002) planes of CQDs [37]. The well-distributed elements Bi, O, Cl and C were found in BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 (Fig. 3e–h and Fig. S2). Therefore, the above results revealed that CQDs and rGO were successfully incorporated with BiOCl. Moreover, according to the thermogravimetric analysis (Fig. S3), the actual content of CQDs in BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 was 2.3 %. And the content of rGO was 9.9 %, which was close to the theoretical value.

XPS was explored to investigate the surface chemical states of as-prepared samples. The survey spectra of BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 (Fig. 4 a) revealed the ternary composite photocatalyst contained Bi, Cl, C, and O elements. In the Bi 4f spectra of BiOCl (Fig. 4b), the binding energy at 159.4 and 164.6 eV belonged to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, revealing Bi3+ in the crystal structure [41]. However, the Bi 4f peaks of BiOCl/CQDs and BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 shifted slightly, which indicated that the Bi3+ chemical environment changed due to the presence of Bi-C interaction in BiOCl/CQDs and BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 [42]. In the high-resolution spectra of Cl 2p of all samples, two intense peaks at 197.9 and 199.6 eV belonged to Cl 2p3/2 and Cl 2p1/2 [43], respectively (Fig. 4c). C1 s spectra (Fig. 4d) revealed different forms of carbon in CQDs and rGO. For BiOCl/CQDs, three peaks at 284.8 [28], 285.7 [44] and 287.3 eV [45] were respectively consistent with CC, CH and COC, indicating the existence of CQDs. For BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10, the binding energy peaks located at 289.0 eV for OC = O corresponded to GO [46]. Besides, the peak of COC in BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 displayed lower intensity than that of GO, proving the partial reduction of GO into rGO. In the O 1s spectra of BiOCl/CQDs and BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 (Fig. 4e), the peaks at 530.4 and 530.5 eV coincided with Bi-O. In BiOCl/CQDs, the other two peaks at 531.9 and 533.4 eV were attributed to CO and OC = O [47]. For BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10, the corresponding peaks were located at 532.2 and 533.1 eV. These results also confirmed oxygen groups-containing rGO and CQDs successfully incorporated into the BiOCl.

The reduction of rGO was further confirmed by Raman. The intensity ratio of D band to G band (ID/IG) could characterize disordered carbon [48,49], which was usually increased with the reduction from GO to rGO. As shown in Fig. 5 , ID/IG of GO was 1.13, which increased to 1.18 for BiOCl/rGO-10, proving the successful reduction from GO to rGO during the hydrothermal process [50]. However, ID/IG for BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 decreased to 1.16 because CQDs also had disordered carbon and influenced the ID/IG ratio [51].

The optical properties of BiOCl, BiOCl/CQDs and BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 were analyzed by UV–vis DRS (Fig. 6 ). It was clearly seen that pure BiOCl only absorbed ultraviolet light, while BiOCl/CQDs and BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 exhibited broad ultraviolet and visible absorption, indicating that both CQDs and rGO could enhanced the light harvesting capability.

Excellent adsorption ability of photocatalysts played an important role in adsorption-photocatalytic removal of pollutant, and the adsorption capacities of the obtained samples were illustrated in Fig. 7 . BiOCl, BiOCl/CQDs and all BiOCl/CQDs/rGO composites could reach adsorption-desorption equilibrium in 60 min. The equilibrium adsorption capacities of BiOCl and BiOCl/CQDs were almost the same, indicating CQDs had no effect on the adsorption ability. However, the adsorption capacities of BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-x (x = 5, 10 and 15) were greatly enhanced with the increase of rGO amount, which were 6.5, 8.0 and 12.0 times of BiOCl, respectively, strongly confirming that introduction of rGO was greatly beneficial to the adsorption property of BiOCl/CQDs/rGO [34,52].

Fig. 9 showed that after the fourth cycle of adsorption-photocatalytic process, the removal efficiency of CIP by BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 just decreased slightly and the crystal structure of the used sample did not change, revealing that BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 exhibited remarkable stability for removal of CIP.

For photocatalysts, the photocurrents (Iph ) depended on the photon absorption rate expressed as current density (Iabs ), charge separation efficiency (ηsep ) and charge injection efficiency (ηinj ), which could be expressed as [63]:
(1)
I
p h
=
I
a b s
×
η
s e p
×
η
i n j

When the photocurrents of samples were measured in solution containing K2S2O8, the value of ηinj was 100 % because K2S2O8 could be used as the electron scavenger and completely suppressed photogenerated carrier recombination on the surface of samples. Therefore Eq. (1) could be simplified as follows:
(2)
I
p h
K S O
=
I
a b s
×
η
s e p

Iabs(BiOCl/CQDs)/Iabs(BiOCl) and Iabs(BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10)/Iabs(BiOCl) were calculated to be 1.1 and 1.3, respectively (calculation details were available in the supplementary materials) [63]. Since the photon absorption rate reflected the effective light absorption, the similar Iabs values of BiOCl, BiOCl/CQDs and BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 indicated that rGO and CQDs could not enhance the effective light absorption although the light harvesting ability was improved significantly (Fig. 6). Therefore, light harvesting enhancement by CQDs and rGO was not a crucial factor for improving the photocatalytic activity. Moreover, Fig. 10 b showed that the photocurrents of BiOCl/CQDs and BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 in K2S2O8-containing solution were 3.0 times and 5.0 times higher than that of BiOCl. According to Eq. (2), the ratios of ηsep between composites and BiOCl were estimated and the results were shown as follows:
η
s e p
B i O C l
/
C Q D s
/
η
s e p
B i O C l
= 2.7
η
s e p ( B i O C l / C Q D s / r G O - 10 )
/
η
s e p ( B i O C l )
= 3.8

It clearly showed that the ηsep of BiOCl/CQDs was higher than that of BiOCl. After incorporating with rGO, the ηsep of BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 was higher than that of BiOCl/CQDs, indicating the simultaneous introduction of CQDs and rGO could significantly accelerate the charge transfer and efficiently suppress the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers.

It could be learned from the Fig. 10a that the photocurrents of BiOCl/CQDs and BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 without electron scavenger were 17.5 times and 41.7 times higher than that of BiOCl. Since the ratios of Iabs and ηsep between composites and pure BiOCl were already obtained, according to Eq. (1), the ratios of ηinj were calculated as follows:
η
i n j
B i O C l
/
C Q D s
/
η
i n j
B i O C l
= 5.9
η
i n j ( B i O C l / C Q D s / r G O - 10 )
/
η
i n j ( B i O C l )
= 8 . 4

Apparently, the charge injection ability of BiOCl/CQDs was improved compared with BiOCl, whereas the charge injection ability of BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 was the best among all of samples, proving the co-introduction of CQDs and rGO was greatly beneficial for charge carrier injection.

The above results demonstrated that despite the cointroduction of rGO and CQDs could enhance the light harvesting ability, it could not improve the effective light absorption. However, the simultaneous introduction of rGO and CQDs remarkably enhanced the separation of charge carriers and the injection of photogenerated electrons because of their excellent electronic conductivities [64,65], leading to outstanding photocatalytic performances.

To further convince the positive roles of CQDs and rGO in enhancing the photocatalytic activity, PL and EIS were analyzed. Fig. 11 a showed that BiOCl/CQDs had lower PL peak intensity than that of BiOCl, while the PL peak intensity of BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 was further lower than that of BiOCl/CQDs, manifesting CQDs and rGO could suppress the recombination of electrons and holes. In addition, EIS results (Fig. 11b) clearly presented that the arcs radii were in order of BiOCl > BiOCl/CQDs > BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10, illustrating incorporated CQDs and rGO were of great benefit to the transportation and separation of photogenerated electrons and holes.

The trapping experiments of BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 were conducted to ascertain the active species in photocatalysis with TEOA, BQ and IPA as the quenchers of h+,
O2 − and
OH, respectively. Fig. 12 presented that the addition of BQ had no influence on photocatalytic efficiency, whereas the addition of TEOA and IPA significantly inhibited the degradation of CIP, indicating that h+ and
OH were the main active species in photodegradation of CIP.

From the results above, the photocatalytic mechanism of BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 could be proposed in Fig. 13 . When irradiated by visible light, the electrons (e−) in the valence band (VB) of BiOCl were excited to the oxygen vacancies states lower than the conduction band (CB), leaving holes (h+) in the VB. Since the VB was more positive than the standard redox potentials of H2O/
OH (2.27 eV) [67], h+ in the VB could react with H2O to form
OH. Thus, h+ and
OH could degrade CIP adsorbed on BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10. Simultaneously, e− in the oxygen vacancies states could rapidly transfer to CQDs and rGO due to the excellent electronic conductivity of CQDs and rGO, thereby effectively accelerating the separation and inhibiting the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers.

To get more insight into the degradation products and pathways of CIP, LC–MS experiments were carried out. According to the identified intermediates and previous reports [68,69], the CIP degradation pathways for BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 were proposed (Fig. 14 ). In pathway 1, the piperazine in CIP (m/z = 332) was attacked by h+ and
OH to formed P1 (m/z = 362). Subsequently, P3 (m/z = 306) was generated by eliminating two carbonyl groups in P1 and then oxidized to P4 (m/z = 291). Then, P5 (m/z = 263) was formed by decarbonylation of P4. In pathway 2, CIP was oxidized into P6 with protonated forms (m/z = 362). Afterwards, P6 transformed into P7 (m/z = 334) by the loss of a carbonyl group. In pathway 3, P8 (m/z = 348) was formed via the attaching of a hydroxyl group to the quinolonic ring. Then P9 (m/z = 288) was generated by dehydroxylation and decarboxylation of P8. Finally, these products might be mineralized to small molecules such as CO2, H2O and NO3 –.

In summary, the ternary BiOCl/CQDs/rGO photocatalyst driven by visible light was obtained by a two-step hydrothermal method. BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 exhibited outstanding removal performance towards CIP owing to the excellent adsorption-photocatalytic property resulting from the introduction of CQDs and rGO. The photon absorption rates of BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 and BiOCl were almost the same, demonstrating CQDs and rGO could not enhance the effective light absorption. While the charge separation efficiency and charge injection efficiency of BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 were much higher than those of BiOCl, strongly proving that CQDs and rGO simultaneously enhanced the separation and the injection of charge carriers. Moreover, rGO significantly improved the adsorption capacity for CIP based on the adsorption experiments results. The BiOCl/CQDs/rGO-10 also displayed high stability and the photocatalytic mechanism of CIP was proposed. This work not only demonstrated the potential application of BiOCl/CQDs/rGO in adsorption-photocatalytic removal of pollutants under visible light, but also provided a deep insight of the roles of CQDs and rGO in enhancing photocatalytic activities for CQDs or rGO based composite photocatalysts.

Xionghui Fu, Yi Zhu and Yuanming Zhang designed the study. Jiehong Huang and Weizhen Chen provided the support on experimental feasibility. Jiehong Huang and Xiang Yu performed characterization and analysis under the supervision of Yi Zhu. Jiehong Huang wrote the paper. Jiehong Huang, Xionghui Fu, Yi Zhu and Yuanming Zhang revised the paper. All authors contributed to the interpretation of the results and improvement of the paper.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Science and Technology Program of Guangzhou (No. 201804010400), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21706091) and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 21617426).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfa.2020.124758.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
